text.title: arch-pattern-hexagonal Failure Modes
text.summary: Migrated antipatterns for arch-pattern-hexagonal as failure modes. Add detector + repair clauses where missing.

failure-mode.description: Defining ports too coarsely — one god-interface with 20 methods that covers everything a service needs.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: God interfaces are hard to mock, hard to swap, and force all adapter implementations to stub irrelevant methods. Split by use case: one port per operation or per coherent group.

failure-mode.description: Defining ports too finely — one interface per method, 50 interfaces for 50 operations.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Death by a thousand mocks. Compose related operations into meaningful port groups; use role-based naming (OrderRepository, not SaveOrderPort).

failure-mode.description: Core service constructor directly instantiating adapters (new PostgresOrderRepository()).
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: This bypasses the port abstraction entirely. The core must receive all dependencies via constructor injection so the DI root can supply in-memory or real adapters.

failure-mode.description: Putting business logic in a primary adapter (e.g., pricing calculation in the REST controller).
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Primary adapters are translation layers only. They convert HTTP/CLI/gRPC input into a port command and return the result. All logic belongs in the core service or domain.
